then.verdict: Rent vs buy is a horizon question — and on a visa, a status question
then.headline: The 5-year test decides most cases; H-1B adds a card most calculators ignore
why: The 5-year test: if you can't confidently see yourself in this city for 5+ years, transaction costs (~8–10% round trip) usually eat any appreciation — rent.
Price-to-rent framing: annual rent × 20 vs purchase price is the honest first filter. Above ~22×, buying needs strong non-financial reasons.

The visa-stability card, said plainly: buying in the US on H-1B means a job loss starts a 60-day clock WHILE you hold a mortgage. That combination — forced job search + forced sale or forced landlording, simultaneously — is the real worst case, and it happens to real people every layoff cycle.

Where should the first crore go: if long-term base is India in 3–5 years, an India purchase with NRI compliance done right often dominates; if US-permanent, US equity + retirement accounts usually come before US property.
pros: Owning is a genuine hedge against rent inflation when the horizon is honest
NRI purchase in India can lock a future base at today's prices
cons: US: property ties you to a metro while your visa ties you to an employer — two anchors, one boat
India: NRI purchases have TDS, repatriation, and POA landmines (checklist covers them)

worst_case.scenario: Layoff on H-1B: 60 days to find a sponsor, a mortgage due monthly, and a market where selling takes 90+ days
worst_case.mitigation: Buy only with: 6+ months of full carrying costs banked, a spouse with independent status or income where possible, and a realistic resale/rental liquidity check on the specific neighborhood

actions.label: NRI purchase checklist
actions.how: Funding trail, POA, TDS-by-seller-type, repatriation — the India-purchase landmines

sources.title: RBI — acquisition of immovable property in India by NRIs (FEMA)
Income Tax India — TDS on property purchase

then.verdict: First crore: India, with the compliance done right
then.headline: If your base is India in 3–5 years, buying your future base beats renting someone else's present
why: You'd be buying the asset you'll actually use — currency risk, repatriation limits, and landlord risk all shrink when the property and the life plan are in the same country.
NRE-funded purchases lock today's rupee prices against your dollar earnings — a real hedge if your income currency is USD and your future expenses are INR.
The compliance list (funding trail, TDS-by-seller, POA) is annoying exactly once — at purchase. Do it right and the asset is clean forever.
pros: Dollar income buying rupee assets is structural tailwind
Family can use/oversee the property meanwhile
cons: Illiquid if plans change — and plans change
Rental yields in India are low (2–3%); this is a use-asset, not an income-asset

worst_case.scenario: Plans change, you stay in the US, and the flat sits as a 2% yield asset with repatriation caps on exit
worst_case.mitigation: Buy only what you'd be happy holding as a family base even if you never move back — location over yield math

then.verdict: First crore: US index funds before US property
then.headline: If your base is the US permanently, fill the tax-advantaged buckets before the down payment
why: The order-of-operations math rarely favors property first: 401(k) match, HSA, Roth/backdoor, then taxable index funds compound without property taxes, maintenance, or a 60-day-clock interaction.
A down payment parked in index funds stays liquid while your green-card timeline resolves — the single biggest unknown in your 5-year test.
Buy when three things align: I-140 approved (or better), 5+ year city confidence, and price-to-rent under ~22× in your metro.
pros: No forced-sale scenario in a layoff
cons: Rent inflation is real — a decade of renting in a hot metro has its own cost
Property forces savings; brokerage accounts require discipline

worst_case.scenario: You wait, your metro appreciates 40%, and the entry price runs away from you
worst_case.mitigation: The same index-fund portfolio participated in much of that growth — you're late to one asset, not poor. Re-run the 5-year test yearly.
